cities.name: Leh
cities.overview: Leh is the cultural and administrative capital of Ladakh, a high-altitude cold desert offering dramatic moon-like landscapes, ancient monasteries, crystal-clear lakes, and thrilling adventure activities. It is one of the most sought-after destinations for travellers seeking raw Himalayan beauty.
cities.history: Once a major stop on the ancient Silk Route, Leh was the royal capital of the independent Himalayan kingdom of Ladakh until the 19th century. It came under Indian administration after 1947 and became the capital of the Union Territory of Ladakh in 2019.

nearest_railhead.name: No direct rail; nearest is Jammu Tawi
nearest_railhead.note: Then road via Srinagar or Manali

places.name: Shanti Stupa
places.description: A prominent Japanese-built white peace pagoda perched on a hill overlooking Leh town. Built in 1985 to promote world peace, it offers breathtaking 360-degree panoramic views of the Indus Valley and surrounding mountains.
places.tips: Climb the 500+ steps early morning or take a taxi. Sunset views are spectacular. Carry water and sunscreen due to high altitude and strong UV rays.

places.name: Leh Palace
places.description: The majestic 17th-century nine-storey palace of the Ladakhi kings, built in Tibetan style and resembling the Potala Palace in Lhasa. It offers stunning views of the old town and Indus River.
places.tips: The views from the roof terrace are breathtaking. Combine with a visit to the nearby Namgyal Tsemo Gompa. Wear warm layers due to altitude and wind.

places.name: Hemis Monastery
places.description: The largest and wealthiest monastery in Ladakh, founded in the 17th century. Famous for its annual Hemis Festival featuring vibrant masked dances (Cham) and ancient thangkas.
places.tips: Visit during the Hemis Festival (June/July) for the grand celebrations. The monastery museum displays rare artefacts and manuscripts. Respectful dress and behaviour required.

places.name: Pangong Lake
places.description: The famous brackish water lake at 4,350m altitude, stretching 134 km across India and China. Known for its mesmerising colour changes from deep blue to turquoise and emerald under different light conditions.
places.tips: Start early for the long scenic drive. Carry oxygen, warm clothes, and motion sickness medicine. The lake changes colour dramatically with sunlight. Permits required for foreigners.

places.name: Nubra Valley (Day Trip)
places.description: The 'Valley of Flowers' with cold desert landscapes, sand dunes at Hunder, Bactrian camels, and the Diskit Monastery with the giant Maitreya Buddha statue.
places.tips: Cross Khardung La (world’s highest motorable pass at 5,359m). Overnight stay recommended for better acclimatisation. Camel safari on the sand dunes is a unique experience.

places.name: Thiksey Monastery
places.description: A magnificent 12-storey monastery resembling the Potala Palace, known for its beautiful murals, statues, and the annual Gustor Festival.
places.tips: Climb to the rooftop for panoramic views. Visit during morning prayers for an authentic experience. The monastery cafe serves good butter tea.

places.name: Magnetic Hill
places.description: A gravity-defying road where vehicles appear to roll uphill due to optical illusion created by the surrounding landscape.
places.tips: Stop for photos and the 'uphill' vehicle demonstration. Combine with a visit to Gurudwara Pathar Sahib nearby.

cities.name: Pangong Tso
cities.overview: Pangong Tso is one of the most beautiful high-altitude brackish lakes in the world, famous for its ever-changing shades of blue, turquoise, and green against the backdrop of barren mountains.
cities.history: Straddling the Line of Actual Control between India and China, the lake has been a silent witness to many historical events and gained global fame after the movie '3 Idiots'.

places.name: Pangong Lake
places.description: The mesmerising 134-km long brackish lake at 4,350m altitude that changes colour from deep blue to turquoise and emerald depending on the sunlight. The Indian side offers stunning views of the Changchenmo Range.
places.tips: Stay overnight in a lakeside camp for magical sunrise and stargazing. Carry oxygen, warm clothes, and motion sickness medicine for the high altitude.

places.name: Spangmik Village
places.description: The last village on the Indian side of Pangong Lake, offering authentic Ladakhi homestays, traditional architecture, and peaceful lakeside walks.
places.tips: Stay in a local homestay to experience Ladakhi culture. The village is perfect for photography and quiet reflection.

cities.name: Nubra Valley
cities.overview: Nubra Valley, also called the 'Valley of Flowers', is famous for its cold desert sand dunes, Bactrian (double-humped) camels, Diskit Monastery, and the dramatic Shyok and Nubra rivers.
cities.history: Part of the ancient Silk Route, Nubra was historically a trade route between Ladakh and Central Asia.

places.name: Hunder Sand Dunes
places.description: Unique cold desert sand dunes in the heart of Nubra Valley where you can ride double-humped Bactrian camels against the backdrop of snow-capped peaks.
places.tips: Camel safari at sunrise or sunset is magical. The dunes are best explored on foot or camel. Carry water and sunscreen.

places.name: Diskit Monastery
places.description: The oldest and largest monastery in Nubra Valley, famous for its giant 32-metre Maitreya Buddha statue and dramatic location on a hill.
places.tips: Climb to the statue for panoramic views. The monastery has beautiful murals and a peaceful atmosphere.

places.name: Panamik Hot Springs
places.description: Natural sulphur hot springs believed to have medicinal properties, located in the remote northern part of Nubra Valley.
places.tips: The water is warm and relaxing. Ideal for sore muscles after long drives. Respect local customs and keep the area clean.

cities.name: Kargil
cities.overview: Kargil is the second-largest town in Ladakh, known for its dramatic landscapes, apricot orchards, Suru Valley, and as the gateway to Zanskar.
cities.history: Historically a trading post on the Leh-Srinagar route, Kargil gained international attention during the 1999 Kargil War.

nearest_railhead.name: No direct rail; nearest is Jammu

places.name: Mulbekh Monastery & Rock Carving
places.description: Famous 8th-century rock carving of Maitreya Buddha and the ancient Mulbekh Gompa perched on a hill overlooking the valley.
places.tips: Stop here while travelling between Kargil and Leh. The carving is visible from the highway. The monastery offers peaceful views.

places.name: Suru Valley
places.description: Stunning valley known for its dramatic peaks including Nun and Kun, lush green fields, and apricot orchards. A photographer’s paradise.
places.tips: The drive is scenic. Best in summer when apricots are ripe. Carry warm layers for higher altitudes.

cities.name: Zanskar Valley
cities.overview: Zanskar is one of the most remote and beautiful valleys in Ladakh, known for its dramatic gorges, ancient monasteries, and the famous Chadar Trek on the frozen Zanskar River.
cities.history: Zanskar was an independent kingdom for centuries and remains a stronghold of Tibetan Buddhist culture.

places.name: Phugtal Monastery
places.description: One of the most spectacular monasteries in the world, built inside a huge cave on a sheer cliff face above the Lungnak River.
places.tips: The trek to Phugtal is challenging but extremely rewarding. Best done with a local guide. Carry sufficient supplies.

cities.name: Lamayuru
cities.overview: Lamayuru is famous for its dramatic 'Moonland' landscape and one of the oldest monasteries in Ladakh, offering a surreal desert-like terrain.
cities.history: The monastery is believed to be over 1,000 years old and was once a major centre of learning.

places.name: Lamayuru Monastery
places.description: One of the oldest and most dramatically located monasteries in Ladakh, perched on a hill surrounded by the unique 'Moonland' eroded landscape.
places.tips: The view from the monastery terrace is unforgettable. Visit during the annual festival for masked dances.
